fields.name: Ben Nevis via CMD Arête
fields.summary: Good mountain paths for much of the route; pathless section on descent with burn crossing. The traverse of the arête is a long but easy scramble.
fields.description: For experienced, fit mountain walkers who do not mind easy scrambling but are not climbers, this is the finest way to climb Ben Nevis. This is a truly spectacular route incorporating two Munros. It will live long in the memory and does true justice to the mountain.

fields.name: Helvellyn via Striding Swirral Edge
fields.summary: The defining route of the Lake District
fields.description: There’s some scrambling involved with hands. Different routes can be taken along Striding Edge depending on the challenge wanted (ie. you can walk to the side of the ridge line for less exposure). The route is fine in good weather conditions for reasonably fit walkers (inc. older children). But beware of doing the Helvellyn route in rain and especially icy/snow conditions – when it becomes very difficult requiring crampons/ice-axe.

fields.name: Tryfan North Face & Bristly Ridge
fields.description: The Tryfan and Bristly Ridge route is a classic, challenging Grade 1 scramble in Snowdonia, typically starting from Ogwen Cottage. The route ascends Tryfan’s North Ridge (often via the North Tower or Heather Terrace), descends to Bwlch Tryfan, scrambles up the exposed Bristly Ridge to Glyder Fach, and usually descends via Y Gribin.

fields.name: Blencathra via Sharp Edge
fields.description: Sharp Edge on Blencathra is a renowned Grade 1 scramble in the Lake District, offering a thrilling, exposed, knife-edge ridge traverse. Composed of slick Skiddaw slate, the narrow, approximately 300-meter-long ridge drops steeply into Scales Tarn, requiring a good head for heights, steady hands-on scrambling, and dry, calm weather conditions.

fields.name: Pavey Ark via Jack's Rake
fields.summary: A steep, narrow, and natural diagonal gully/rake slicing across the rock face.
fields.description: Jack’s Rake is a thrilling, exposed Grade 1 scramble up the center of Pavey Ark in the Lake District, offering 700 meters of challenging, direct ascent with significant exposure. It is a popular, natural diagonal gully—visible from Stickle Tarn—involving steep rock, mossy sections, and loose rock, suitable for experienced scramblers.

fields.name: Snowdon via Crib Goch
fields.summary: Extreme on Crib Goch; not for those uncomfortable with heights.
fields.description: The Snowdon Horseshoe is a challenging, 7-8 mile (11-12 km) Grade 1 scramble loop starting at Pen-y-Pass, featuring extreme exposure, narrow ridges, and roughly 900m-1000m of ascent. It traverses Crib Goch, Garnedd Ugain, Snowdon (Yr Wyddfa), and Y Lliwedd, offering a thrilling, classic mountaineering day in 6-8 hours.

fields.name: Blencathra via Hall's Fell Ridge
fields.summary: A "knife-edge" feel in the upper sections, described as a series of knobbly humps and rock towers known as the "Narrow Edge".
fields.description: Hall's Fell Ridge is a direct, steep, and thrilling Grade 1 scramble leading to the summit of Blencathra in the Lake District, frequently described as the finest way to ascend the mountain. It offers a rocky, exposed, and narrow ascent with spectacular views, starting from Threlkeld. While easier than Sharp Edge, it still requires scrambling skills.

fields.name: Ring of Steall
fields.summary: Grade 1 scrambling, involving steep, exposed, and narrow rock arêtes.
fields.description: The Ring of Steall is a classic, strenuous 10-mile (16km) circular ridge walk in the Mamores, near Fort William, Scotland, featuring four Munros (An Gearanach, Stob Coire a' Chairn, Am Bodach, and Sgùrr a' Mhaim). It features dramatic, exposed scrambling—including the famous "Devil's Ridge"—and typically takes 8-10 hours, offering spectacular views of Ben Nevis and the Steall Falls.

fields.name: Liathach
fields.summary: The full traversal is a major, serious, and exposed mountaineering day (often considered a top-three UK ridge).
fields.description: Liathach is a towering, stegosaurus-like mountain massif in Torridon, Northwest Highlands, widely considered one of Scotland’s most spectacular and challenging, rocky traverses. It is known as "The Grey One," featuring a 10km ridge with two 1,000m+ Munros (Spidean a' Choire Lèith and Mullach an Rathain) connected by the airy, Grade 2 Am Fasarinen pinnacles.

fields.name: Great Gable
fields.summary: Challenging/Hard, requiring good navigation, fitness, and, in some sections, hands-on rock scrambling.
fields.description: Great Gable (899m) is a iconic, pyramid-shaped Lake District fell offering panoramic views. Popular routes start from Honister Pass (approx. 6 miles) or Seathwaite (approx. 7 miles), featuring steep, rocky ascents, loose scree, and potential scrambling. The summit, often accessed via Windy Gap, holds a notable WWI war memorial plaque.

fields.name: Suilven
fields.summary: Minor scrambling is required, with a sharper Grade 2 step required for some approaches, particularly near the Eastern Top.
fields.description: Suilven is a strenuous 20–23km (12-14 mile) round-trip hike in Assynt, Scotland, typically taking 6–9 hours. The most popular route starts from Glencanisp Lodge near Lochinver, involving a long, remote approach through boggy moorland followed by a steep ascent to a narrow ridge with panoramic views and minor scrambling.

fields.name: Buachaille Etive Mòr
fields.summary: A dramatic, narrow ridge containing four main summits, with Stob Dearg being the most iconic peak.
fields.description: Buachaille Etive Mòr ("Big Herdsman of Etive") is an iconic, pyramid-shaped mountain ridge in the Scottish Highlands at the head of Glen Etive. Known for its steep cliffs and two Munros, Stob Dearg (1,022m) and Stob na Broige (956m), it is a classic hiking and scrambling destination, famous for its dramatic, triangular appearance and being a frequent, popular, and challenging walk from Altnafeadh.

fields.name: An Teallach
fields.summary: The traverse is notoriously exposed, requiring a head for heights and solid scrambling ability.
fields.description: An Teallach is a dramatic, rocky mountain in Wester Ross, Scotland, best known for its challenging 600m sandstone pinnacle ridge traverse. The classic route, often done anti-clockwise from Dundonnell, includes two Munros, Bidein a’ Ghlas Thuill and Sgùrr Fiòna, covering approx. 13-15km with over 1,300m of ascent. It requires serious scrambling and careful navigation, particularly over the Corrag Bhuidhe pinnacles.

fields.name: Bowfell via Climbers’ Traverse
fields.summary: A narrow terrace path providing dramatic views and a close-up experience of the steep Bowfell cliffs, specifically passing below Cambridge Crag.
fields.description: The Climber’s Traverse on Bowfell is a classic, non-technical, yet atmospheric path in the Lake District, offering a dramatic alternative to The Band ridge path. It traverses the base of Bowfell's steep Eastern crags (Flat Crags/Cambridge Crag), passing a notable water spring under the buttresses, and leads to the iconic Great Slab for a unique ascent.

fields.name: Scafell Pike via Lord's Rake
fields.summary: Technical in places; not suitable for beginners and dangerous in wet, icy, or high-wind conditions
fields.description: Lord’s Rake is a challenging Grade 1 scramble, providing a dramatic, direct route up Sca Fell (964m) before crossing to Scafell Pike. Starting from Wasdale, it is a narrow, steep rock gully filled with loose scree, requiring, using hands and feet, particularly for three steep sections. A helmet is strongly recommended due to risks of falling rock.

fields.name: Kinder Scout via Red Brook
fields.summary: The route tracks a steep brook, turning into a rocky scramble up a gully. It is considered one of the longest, most technical, yet accessible scrambles in the area, offering fun,, rocky moves with opportunities to bypass the toughest parts.
fields.description: The Red Brook scramble to Kinder Scout is a popular, scenic Grade 1 scramble starting from Hayfield/Kinder Reservoir, offering a direct, adventurous ascent to the plateau. It features a rocky, steep gully with cascading water and impressive views, serving as a challenging yet beginner-friendly route to the Peak District's highest point

fields.name: Ingleborough
fields.summary: Moderate to challenging; requires good fitness for steep, rough, and exposed sections.
fields.description: The classic 10-mile (17 km) circular walk to Ingleborough (723m) from Clapham in the Yorkshire Dales is highly varied, taking 5-6 hours. Highlights include the village of Clapham, Trow Gill gorge, Gaping Gill pothole, and dramatic, expansive views from the flat-topped summit.

fields.name: Crinkle Crags
fields.summary: Crinkle Crags by name and nature, a rugged jumble of shattered rocky towers on a ridge set across the head of Oxendale at the far end of the Langdale valley
fields.description: The Crinkle Crags route from Great Langdale is a challenging ~12-13km (7.9-mile) walk involving around 850-1000m of ascent, usually taking 4-6 hours. Typically starting from the Old Dungeon Ghyll Hotel, the route ascends via The Band, tackles the five distinct rocky summits, passes the "Bad Step," and offers panoramic views before returning, often combined with Bowfell.
